title: The 'Nothing Ever Happens' Era Just Ended — And It's Not Coming Back

source.original: 'Nothing Ever Happens' Is Over: The Fully Interconnected Startup — Naval Podcast

body_html:
For years, a prevailing sentiment in geopolitics and technology circles was that despite all the noise, the global order was fundamentally stable. Markets absorbed shocks, institutions held, and the phrase nothing ever happens became a shorthand for a kind of comfortable stasis. Naval Ravikant argues that this era is now definitively over.

The convergence of exponential technologies — AI, drones, biotech, decentralized coordination tools — has created a world where disruption isn't a buzzword but a lived reality unfolding across multiple domains simultaneously. The old mental model of slow, incremental change punctuated by rare black swans no longer applies. Instead, interconnected systems amplify and accelerate shocks in ways that are difficult to predict or contain.

This reframing matters because it changes how entrepreneurs, investors, and policymakers should allocate attention. If the baseline assumption shifts from stability with occasional disruption to continuous disruption as the new normal, then resilience, adaptability, and speed become existential priorities rather than nice-to-haves.

The ancient phrase may you live in interesting times — traditionally understood as a curse — is recast here as a precise description of the present moment. We are living through a period where the upside potential and downside risk of technology are both expanding at exponential rates, and the gap between the two defines the stakes of the decade ahead.
